title: Single plasmon transport in one dimensional nanowire
authors: A. A. Dıaz-Valles, B. Rousseaux, S. Guérin, H. Jauslin, A. Leray, G. Colas des Francs
abstract: We introduce a unified theoretical framework for single-plasmon transport in one-dimensional nanowires, bridging the quantized electromagnetic Green's tensor formalism with effective non-Hermitian Hamiltonian models. This approach naturally incorporates propagating surface plasmon polaritons, high-order modes dissipative channels, and intrinsic losses. We investigate both the stationary regime and the spatio-temporal dynamics of a single-plasmon pulse travelling through an atomic chain coupled to a dispersive nanowire. We analyze modal contributions to reflection and transmission spectra for quantum emitter coupled to a silver nanowire, a configuration proposed as a single-plasmon transistor, and we demonstrate that optimized multi-emitter systems offer significant advantages. In case of one quantum emitter coupled to a silver nanowire at telecom wavelengths, we predict a single-plasmon transmittivity down to 7\% under realistic conditions, and an atomic qubit population of 12\%. Extension to multi-emitter systems using a Löwdin orthogonalization procedure enables a consistent treatment of collective interactions. We show that optimized positioning with just five emitters enhances plasmon modulation, achieving a transmittivity of 2\% but also reduces coupling losses to one-third compared to the single-emitter case. Our results establish a robust foundation for analyzing and designing plasmonic waveguide quantum electrodynamics systems.
summary: This paper establishes a comprehensive theoretical framework to analyze how single plasmons transport through nanowires coupled to multiple quantum emitters. By unifying Green's tensor methods with non-Hermitian Hamiltonians, the authors predict that optimizing emitter placement enhances plasmon control and reduces energy losses significantly. The results provide a robust foundation for designing next-generation plasmonic quantum devices.
main_problem: The research develops a unified theoretical framework to model single-plasmon transport through one-dimensional nanowires coupled to quantum emitters.
main_result: Optimized multi-emitter systems significantly enhance plasmon modulation, achieving high transmittivities while substantially reducing coupling losses compared to single-emitter setups.
method: The authors use a combination of quantized electromagnetic Green's tensor formalism and effective non-Hermitian Hamiltonian models to solve coupled differential equations in both time and frequency domains.
model_or_system: The system involves quantum emitters (like atomic qubits) coupled to a dispersive metallic nanowire, such as silver, operating at telecom wavelengths. The model incorporates the emitter energy ($\hat{H}_{QE}$), field energy ($\hat{H}_F$), and interaction ($\hat{H}_{int}$).
key_observables: Single-plasmon transmittivity ($T_0$), reflectivity ($R_0$), atomic qubit population, and high-order mode losses ($Q_{abs}$).
important_parameters: Telecom wavelengths, single/multi-emitter count (e.g., 5 emitters), coupling rates ($\Gamma_n$), and frequency shifts ($\Omega_n$).
main_assumptions: Approximations include the rotating-wave approximation and assuming slow variation of coupling strength over the pulse bandwidth.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schemes for emitter coupling, modal reflectivity/transmittivity spectra ($R_0$ vs $T_0$), time evolution of excitation pulses showing interference dips, and dynamics of excited state populations for multiple emitters.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the unified theoretical framework (Green's tensor to non-Hermitian Hamiltonian) to deriving scattering amplitudes, analyzing single-emitter performance, extending the model using Löwdin orthonormalization for multi-emitters, and finally solving the long-time limit of the transport coefficients.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it bridges cavity QED concepts (Hamiltonian modeling, coupling strengths) with solid-state photonics (plasmonic waveguides), providing quantitative predictions for controlling quantum light-matter interactions in nanoscale devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
